sentences.target_language: SethはKaylaに「お腹空いた？」と聞きました。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa Kayla ni "onaka suita?" to kikimashita.

sentences.base_language: kayla says, "yes, I am very hungry"
sentences.target_language: Kaylaは「うん、すごくお腹空いてる」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "un, sugoku onaka suiteru" to iimashita.

sentences.base_language: Seth asks, "do you want to eat tacos"
sentences.target_language: Sethは「タコス食べたい？」と聞きました。
sentences.grammarNote: 〜たい (tai) attaches to the verb stem to express desire: 食べたい = 'want to eat'. In casual speech, the desu is often dropped, making it 食べたい？ rather than 食べたいですか？

sentences.base_language: Kayla says, "hmm, i ate tacos yesterday"
sentences.target_language: Kaylaは「うーん、昨日タコス食べたんだよね」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "uun, kinou takosu tabetan da yo ne" to iimashita.
sentences.literal: Kayla [topic] 「hmm, yesterday tacos ate [explanatory] is [emphasis] [seeking-agreement]」 [quote] said.
sentences.grammarNote: んだよね (n da yo ne) is a casual combination: ん (explanatory softener) + だ (copula) + よ (assertion) + ね (seeking agreement). Together they convey 'I mean... I ate tacos yesterday, you know?' — a very natural spoken Japanese pattern.

sentences.base_language: Seth asks, "do you want to cook spaghetti"
sentences.target_language: Sethは「スパゲッティ作りたい？」と聞きました。
Kaylaは「うん、スパゲッティ大好き」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "un, supagetti daisuki" to iimashita.

sentences.base_language: seth says, "okay, we need to buy ingredients."
sentences.target_language: Sethは「じゃあ、材料を買わないとね」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa "jaa, zairyou wo kawanai to ne" to iimashita.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜ないと (nai to) is a casual shortening of 〜ないといけない: 'have to / must'. 買わないとね = 'we've gotta buy [them], you know'. The full form 買わないといけない means 'it won't do if we don't buy'.

sentences.target_language: Kaylaは「何が必要？」と聞きました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "nani ga hitsuyou?" to kikimashita.

sentences.base_language: Seth responds, "We need a tomato, onion, garlic, herbs, and noodles"
sentences.target_language: Sethは「トマト、玉ねぎ、にんにく、ハーブ、それと麺が必要だよ」と答えました。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa "tomato, tamanegi, ninniku, haabu, sore to men ga hitsuyou da yo" to kotaemashita.
sentences.literal: Seth [topic] 「tomato, onion, garlic, herbs, and noodles [subj] necessary is [emphasis]」 [quote] answered.

sentences.base_language: Kayla says, "but the store is closed."
sentences.target_language: Kaylaは「でもお店閉まってるよ」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "demo omise shimatteru yo" to iimashita.

sentences.base_language: They both look for the ingredients in their house.
sentences.target_language: 二人は家の中で材料を探しました。
sentences.romanized: futari wa ie no naka de zairyou wo sagashimashita.

sentences.base_language: Seth finds three tomatoes, one onion, but no noodles.
sentences.target_language: Sethはトマト三個と玉ねぎ一個を見つけましたが、麺はありませんでした。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa tomato sanko to tamanegi ikko wo mitsukemashita ga, men wa arimasen deshita.
sentences.literal: Seth [topic] tomatoes three-[objects] and onion one-[object] [obj] found but, noodles [topic] did-not-exist.

sentences.base_language: Seth says, "damnit, we don't have noodles"
sentences.target_language: Sethは「くそ、麺がないや」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa "kuso, men ga nai ya" to iimashita.

sentences.base_language: Kayla says, "but we have pizza crust.  We can make pizza sauce!"
sentences.target_language: Kaylaは「でもピザ生地があるよ。ピザソースを作れるじゃん！」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Kayla wa "demo piza kiji ga aru yo. piza soosu wo tsukureru jan!" to iimashita.
sentences.literal: Kayla [topic] 「but pizza dough [subj] exists [emphasis]. pizza sauce [obj] can-make [right]!」 [quote] said.
sentences.grammarNote: じゃん (jan) is a casual sentence-final particle that asserts something the speaker considers obvious, similar to 'right?' or 'isn't it?'. It's a contracted form of じゃないか used mainly in casual Tokyo speech.

sentences.base_language: Seth and Kayla start to cook.
sentences.target_language: SethとKaylaは料理を始めました。
sentences.romanized: Seth to Kayla wa ryouri wo hajimemashita.

sentences.base_language: after some time they eat the pizza.
sentences.target_language: しばらくして、二人はピザを食べました。
sentences.romanized: shibaraku shite, futari wa piza wo tabemashita.

sentences.target_language: ピザはとても美味しかったです。

sentences.base_language: Seth looks at Kayla and says, "this is good but I like tacos better".
sentences.target_language: SethはKaylaを見て「これ美味しいけど、やっぱりタコスの方が好きだな」と言いました。
sentences.romanized: Seth wa Kayla wo mite "kore oishii kedo, yappari takosu no hou ga suki da na" to iimashita.
sentences.literal: Seth [topic] Kayla [obj] seeing 「this delicious but, after-all tacos [possessive] direction [subj] like is [soft-assertion]」 [quote] said.
sentences.grammarNote: 〜の方が (no hou ga) means 'the [X] side' in a comparison: タコスの方が好き = 'I like tacos more'. より (yori) marks the thing being compared against, but here it's implied — the preference for tacos over pizza is understood from context.

sentences.base_language: They argue about that then go to bed.
sentences.target_language: 二人はそのことで言い合ってから、寝ました。
sentences.romanized: futari wa sono koto de iiatte kara, nemashita.
sentences.literal: Two-people [topic] that matter [means] argued and-then, slept.
